chain_of_thought: Native stack shows crash in Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum, called from certificate parsing/ASN.1 conversion routines within libUbikeyJni.so. This is application code, not allocator or sanitizer. Because I cannot access Ghidra/Jadx endpoints (404 from tools), I must reason from symbols and context only, but still follow the required schema.

Function names strongly suggest an internal ASN.1 parser / X.509 certificate loader: CERT_Load → Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT → Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT → Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum. Crashes in ASN.1 child-number computation are classically where length / tag parsing bugs or out-of-bounds indexing occur. These are frequently security issues in TLS/PKI stacks when given malicious certificates.

fuzz_one_input is the harness entry and calls Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetSubjectName. In the real app, that JNI method likely loads or parses a certificate (from a smart card, file, or network) and then returns the subject name. Thus, the inputs it processes (ASN.1-encoded certificates) are plausibly attacker-controlled via an external certificate, smartcard content, or remote data. Fuzzing has found that some malformed ASN.1 input can cause a crash in Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum.

Since I cannot inspect code, I cannot prove the exact bug (e.g., out-of-bounds read vs. null deref), but a dedicated function Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum almost certainly walks ASN.1 tree structures computing child element counts or indexes based on header/length fields. A crash here under malformed input points to insufficient bounds checks on ASN.1 structures derived entirely from untrusted bytes passed into CERT_Load. This is a textbook pattern for memory safety bugs in parsers.

There is no indication the crash is due solely to the fuzz harness violating JNI contracts (e.g., always passing NULL env or invalid jbyteArray); the harness calls a normal exported JNI function which then goes deep into parsing logic. Errors in ASN.1 parsing that lead to process termination are not harness-only by default; a production caller could also supply malformed/hostile certs unless there is strong validation before JNI (which I cannot confirm).

Given lack of code visibility, I cannot precisely assign a CWE, but most likely candidates are out-of-bounds read/write or integer/length miscalculation during ASN.1 child count computation. Even if it were "only" an assertion-based abort, the fact that it occurs in certificate parsing reachable from untrusted certs implies at least a practical denial-of-service. Because of strong prior knowledge of ASN.1 parser bugs, I assess this as likely a real vulnerability with medium confidence (~0.65), and at least medium severity (DoS / potential memory corruption).

I cannot derive an exact Java callgraph or manifest entry, so exploit details must stay high-level and assume that passing a malicious cert into jGetSubjectName is possible (e.g., by importing a cert or interacting with a smart card).
reasons: Crash occurs in application ASN.1 parsing logic (Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum) rather than in harness-only glue
Call chain shows a structured certificate load/parse path (CERT_Load → Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT → …) typically operating on attacker-controllable certificates
Malformed ASN.1 structures commonly trigger out-of-bounds or length-calculation bugs in such functions, leading to memory corruption or at least process-terminating faults
Fuzzing entrypoint simply calls the same JNI that production Java would use, indicating the crash is not obviously due to unrealistic JNI misuse
recommendations: Manually review Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum, Convert_ASN1_to_X509_* and CERT_Load for improper length/tag validation and out-of-bounds access on ASN.1 structures
Add strict bounds checks and centralized ASN.1 decoding helpers that verify all lengths and child counts before dereferencing or indexing
Instrument this path with ASan/UBSan and create regression fuzz targets around CERT_Load and jGetSubjectName with realistic certificate sources
assumptions: jGetSubjectName processes ASN.1/X.509 certificate data ultimately derived from attacker-supplied or semi-trusted sources
The crash is due to malformed ASN.1 data rather than an artificial harness misuse of JNI (e.g., passing invalid JNIEnv)
No comprehensive input validation is performed in Java before calling the native code
limitations: Unable to decompile native functions or Java methods, so the exact failing instruction and memory operation are unknown
No concrete crash log (signal, address, register state) was provided, limiting precise classification of read vs write vs assertion
The real-world path by which untrusted certificates reach jGetSubjectName is not confirmed

exploit.trigger_method: Supplying a crafted ASN.1/X.509 certificate to the code path that calls UbikeyJni.jGetSubjectName
prerequisites: Attacker can cause the application to load or parse an untrusted X.509 certificate (file, smartcard, or network-provided)
Application exposes or uses UbikeyJni.jGetSubjectName on data not fully controlled by the app
Target device allows installation or interaction with the vulnerable app
exploit_pipeline: Craft an ASN.1/X.509 certificate with malformed length or structure fields designed to stress child-number computation in the ASN.1 tree
Deliver this certificate to the application via the mechanism it uses (e.g., importing a certificate file or interacting with a smartcard or remote service)
Ensure the application invokes UbikeyJni.jGetSubjectName on this certificate, triggering CERT_Load and subsequent ASN.1 parsing
Observe application crash or abnormal behavior when Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum operates on the malformed structure
exploit.notes: The exact mechanism for feeding the crafted certificate to jGetSubjectName depends on the app’s UI or IPC. The PoC sequence assumes an activity that accepts a certificate path and eventually calls into UbikeyJni.jGetSubjectName.

chain_of_thought: Top native frame is __memcpy (allocator/libc); first app-level frame below that is Decode_DER_ASN1_ST in libUbikeyJni.so per stack trace.
Crash in __memcpy strongly suggests an out-of-bounds read/write or overlap/size issue while decoding DER ASN.1 structures.

Call chain inside native: Decode_DER_ASN1_ST -> Get_DER_Child -> Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT -> CERT_Load -> Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetSubjectName; Java / fuzzer entry is fuzz_one_input -> Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetSubjectName.

Even though I couldn’t decompile via MCP due to 404 errors, the function names are descriptive: CERT_Load and Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT almost certainly parse a DER-encoded X.509 certificate or ASN.1 blob, and Decode_DER_ASN1_ST / Get_DER_Child imply walking child nodes in ASN.1.
Memcpys inside ASN.1 decoders are classic sources of memory corruption if length fields in the encoded data are trusted without validating against buffer boundaries or overall input size.

The fuzzer harness fuzz_one_input is calling the JNI entry; in the real app this JNI method will be fed by some untrusted or semi-untrusted input (e.g., certificate from server, NFC, file, etc.). Lengths in DER are attacker-controlled by design.

Because the crash is in memcpy and immediately preceded by Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, the most plausible mechanism is that Decode_DER_ASN1_ST (or a callee) uses a size derived from DER length fields without robust bounds checking relative to the original buffer length, leading to memcpy over-read or overwrite.
There is no indication this is an intentional abort, harness-only misuse (e.g., passing null pointers) — it’s a regular memcpy crash while decoding structured input.

Out-of-bounds memcpy on attacker-influenced data is a memory safety bug and likely exploitable (heap overflow or read) depending on direction; at minimum it’s a serious DoS and potential information disclosure / RCE vector in native code.
Due to MCP limitations I cannot show exact code snippets, but the stack and symbols give strong evidence for a classic parsing-length-vs-buffer mismatch vulnerability in the ASN.1/X.509 parsing path.
reasons: Crash occurs in __memcpy during ASN.1/X.509 certificate decoding path (Decode_DER_ASN1_ST → Get_DER_Child → Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT).
ASN.1 DER length fields are attacker-controlled and commonly mishandled, leading to incorrect memcpy length calculations.
No signs this is a harness-only issue; fuzz harness simply replaces real Java caller, using same JNI method and parsing logic.
An unchecked or insufficiently checked memcpy based on decoded DER lengths implies an out-of-bounds read/write memory corruption.
recommendations: Manually audit Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, Get_DER_Child, and Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT for all memcpy/memmove/memset calls and enforce strict bounds checks against the original buffer length.
Add centralized DER parsing utilities that validate length fields (including nested/constructed types) before any copying, rejecting malformed or overlong structures.
Enable hardened allocator / ASan builds for this library during testing to localize exact overflow site and add regression tests with malformed ASN.1 inputs.
assumptions: Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetSubjectName takes a byte array or buffer containing DER-encoded certificate or ASN.1 data from higher-level app logic.
Decode_DER_ASN1_ST or its callees are responsible for computing memcpy sizes from DER length fields without complete validation.
Fuzzer is not doing anything impossible for production, just giving malformed but syntactically plausible ASN.1 blobs.
limitations: Could not decompile libUbikeyJni.so via MCP due to tooling HTTP 404 errors; classification is based on stack trace and symbol names only.
Exact memcpy call site, arguments, and whether overflow is read vs write are unknown.
Java-side call graph and argument validation were not inspected due to missing Jadx context.

exploit.trigger_method: Malformed ASN.1 / X.509 certificate parsed via Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetSubjectName JNI call
prerequisites: Ability to supply or influence DER-encoded certificate or ASN.1 data processed by the app (e.g., as remote server cert, file, or message payload).
Target device running the vulnerable version of com.skt.smartbill with libUbikeyJni.so.
No additional sandboxing that would neutralize native memory corruption.
exploit_pipeline: Craft a DER-encoded certificate or ASN.1 blob with manipulated length fields such that Decode_DER_ASN1_ST computes an oversized length for a memcpy operation.
Deliver this malformed blob through whichever app feature ultimately calls UbikeyJni.jGetSubjectName (e.g., import certificate, open smart bill, or similar).
When the app invokes Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetSubjectName, CERT_Load and Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT process the blob, reaching Decode_DER_ASN1_ST.
The unchecked memcpy overruns heap or stack buffers, causing memory corruption that can be probed and refined for control-flow hijack (RCE) or at minimum denial-of-service.
exploit.notes: Exact feature and Intent extras to reach UbikeyJni.jGetSubjectName are unknown; PoC commands use placeholder package/activity names and a certificate path extra that should be adapted after inspecting the actual app flows.

chain_of_thought: Native stack shows crash in __memcpy called from Decode_DER_ASN1_ST → CERT_Load → JNI jGetSubjectName, invoked by generic fuzz_one_input harness, so first app-level native frame below libc is Decode_DER_ASN1_ST/CERT_Load in libUbikeyJni.so.

Tool access to Ghidra APIs failed (404) so I cannot decompile the functions or inspect memcpy call sites; likewise I cannot perform full backward dataflow or inspect src/dst/len expressions, and I also lack Jadx Java-side context beyond the bridge method name and that it’s a standard JNI entry in the main app library.

Despite missing decompilation, some structural facts are clear: CERT_Load and Decode_DER_ASN1_ST are certificate/ASN.1 parsing routines; decoding DER ASN.1 commonly involves copying length-prefixed fields from untrusted certificate blobs into internal structures. A memcpy crash in this context strongly suggests an out-of-bounds read/write or buffer overflow driven by parsed length values.

The fuzz harness ‘fuzz_one_input’ likely feeds arbitrary data (emulating certificates or blobs) into CERT_Load (directly or via JNI). In the real app, these certificate blobs would be influenced by external input (server certificates, smartcard responses, QR payloads, etc.), which are realistically attacker-controlled or at least untrusted.

Because I cannot see validation, I must reason qualitatively: robust DER parsers must rigorously bounds-check lengths; fuzzing crashed in __memcpy, meaning some memcpy was invoked with invalid pointers/lengths. This is classic vulnerable behavior in ASN.1 decoders when length checks are insufficient or integer math overflows. It is unlikely to be a harness-only artifact because the memcpy resides inside app library parsing code, not in the fuzzer wrapper, and the path is via normal JNI entry ‘jGetSubjectName’ that would be used in production.

Thus, there is strong circumstantial evidence of a memory safety bug (likely out-of-bounds read/write) in Decode_DER_ASN1_ST/CERT_Load when processing DER/ASN.1 data; however, without code I cannot definitively state if the memcpy is always bounded to the input buffer, or if harness passed entirely unrealistic arguments (e.g., NULL buffers) that real app never would. Still, fuzzing’s goal is to simulate malformed inputs, which are realistic for external certificates, so I treat this as a likely genuine vulnerability.

Impact-wise, a memcpy overflow in a JNI certificate parsing routine can allow memory corruption inside the app process, typically leading to high-severity issues (potential RCE under some conditions or at least process compromise). I therefore classify as vulnerable with high (but not absolute) confidence, severity high, and describe exploitation at a conceptual level (malformed certificate / ASN.1 blob delivered via whichever channel jGetSubjectName uses).
reasons: Crash is in __memcpy called from ASN.1/DER decoding and certificate loading routines (Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, CERT_Load) inside the app’s JNI library, a common hotspot for length-check bugs.

Fuzzer feeds arbitrary input through JNI jGetSubjectName into CERT_Load, emulating untrusted certificate/identity blobs that are realistically attacker-controlled in production (e.g., remote certs, tokens).

A memcpy-based crash in a decoder strongly suggests out-of-bounds access due to insufficient validation of attacker-controlled length/offset fields rather than a benign abort; no evidence suggests the failure is solely due to a contrived harness misuse.
JNI bridge and library map show this is normal app-native code (libUbikeyJni.so), not a harness-only library, so the same code path is likely reachable in normal app operation.
recommendations: Decompile CERT_Load and Decode_DER_ASN1_ST to locate all memcpy/memmove calls and add strict bounds checks on source and destination based on the actual buffer sizes.
Add comprehensive length and nesting validation to the DER/ASN.1 parsing logic (including integer overflow checks on length arithmetic) before any memory copy operations.
Re-run fuzzing with ASan/UBSan enabled on libUbikeyJni.so and record precise crash sites to confirm and fix all similar issues in the certificate/ASN.1 decoding stack.
assumptions: jGetSubjectName processes externally supplied, potentially attacker-controlled certificate or identity data (e.g., from network or token).
The fuzzer’s fuzz_one_input simulates realistic malformed ASN.1/DER payloads, not impossible NULL-only arguments that production code would never see.
The memcpy crash is due to invalid size/pointer parameters derived from parsed input fields rather than an unrelated libc or OS issue.
limitations: Ghidra MCP access failed (HTTP 404), so I could not decompile CERT_Load, Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, or inspect the exact memcpy arguments.
I could not inspect the Java implementation of UbikeyJni.jGetSubjectName to see how inputs are constructed or validated.
No register/memory context from the crash (e.g., actual length values) is available, so the exact nature (over-read vs over-write) of the memory corruption remains unknown.

exploit.trigger_method: Supply a crafted DER/ASN.1 certificate or identity blob that is processed via UbikeyJni.jGetSubjectName (e.g., through whatever higher-level API triggers subject-name extraction).
prerequisites: Attacker can cause the app to parse an attacker-controlled or at least malleable certificate/ASN.1 structure via the UbikeyJni.jGetSubjectName path.
The vulnerable memcpy in Decode_DER_ASN1_ST/CERT_Load is reachable with those inputs in production builds.
Target device allows installation or interaction with the vulnerable app version.
exploit_pipeline: Craft a malformed DER/ASN.1 payload with inconsistent length fields designed to cause an out-of-bounds memcpy in Decode_DER_ASN1_ST (e.g., overlong length vs actual buffer).
Deliver the crafted payload through the app’s normal interface that eventually invokes UbikeyJni.jGetSubjectName (for example, by presenting a malicious certificate or token).
When the app calls CERT_Load and Decode_DER_ASN1_ST on the payload, the unchecked length is used as memcpy size, corrupting adjacent heap memory in libUbikeyJni.so.
Iterate payload adjustments guided by crashes (e.g., via logcat/ASan) to attempt controlled memory corruption and, in a stronger scenario, code execution within the app process.
poc_commands: adb shell am start -n com.skt.smartbill/.MainActivity --es cert_path /sdcard/Download/crafted_cert.der  # assumes the app accepts a certificate path to trigger jGetSubjectName
exploit.notes: Exact triggering UI/component and file paths are unknown; the PoC commands assume there is an activity (e.g., MainActivity) and an intent extra that causes the app to load and parse a certificate, eventually invoking UbikeyJni.jGetSubjectName. These details must be aligned with the actual Java call graph and manifest.
